text:
In the past twenty years, international criminal justice has undergone a profound evolution. After decades of systematic impunity, international criminal justice institutions have undergone a process of rapid institutionalization and judicialization through the creation of international and hybrid criminal courts and tribunals. From a standing start, these institutions have rapidly become the central institutional actors in the prosecution of international crimes such as genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes. From a jurisprudential perspective, it proved necessary for international criminal courts to address almost immediately an avalanche of trial activities dealing with highly contested facts in deeply emotive situations and raising numerous legal issues in situations where no national court had yet had an opportunity to develop judicial doctrine and practice.

Recently, a number of constitutional court decisions have been put under the microscope in the public domain, with even some members of the Constitutional Court itself questioning their constitutional validity. Highly contentious disputes have been playing out in the public domain, resulting in significant criticism of the judiciary as being lacking in efficiency and justice. This article will focus on the jurisprudential rules and methodology applied in international criminal law and how the international criminal justice system is under increasing pressure from both internal and external challenges and tensions emanating from the very conflicts that it seeks to prosecute.
